originalTitle: France 4-6 England: World Cup third-place playoff – as it happened
summary: Bukayo Saka scored a hat-trick before Jude Bellingham clinched a thriller as England claimed bronze
content: So well done, England, and adieu Didier Deschamps, one of the greatest international coaches in history. The England players are being presented with their bronze medals. Bronze is coming home. Jordan Henderson, the master of ceremonies, calls Jason Steele, the reserve-reserve keeper on, and presents him with a medal. Thomas Tuchel also spoke to the BBC: “We had a brilliant first half and then a turbulent second half. You could see the difference of what one day makes and the difference in the schedule. We are so, so tired and drained from the last weeks. “So a massive compliment and full respect - the mentality that we showed. Going through all the adversity is absolutely brilliant. I was afraid of the physical demands of the match. We know the quality and speed of France, and they have much less schedule for them. It was less demanding. “They had one more day in-between the semi-final to digest, and they had way less travel distances than us. We played in the heat, at altitude. I was worried physically. You could see it in the second half, the cramps and all the tiredness, but I was never worried about the mentality. “I have said it before, this team has created something very special, and they showed it again.” Bukayo Saka spoke afterwards to the BBC: “It was a crazy, crazy game. We’re both still quite disappointed to not be in the final but it was about finishing strong and for us giving the country the best finish in the World Cup in 60 years, so we’re happy with the final result. “I think the first half went for us. We won the first half, they won the second half and in the end we got the two goals to win the game, so I’d say that’s how the game went.” “Jude was never taking it. He was the first one to say go and get your hat-trick, so none of them came to distract me. I was always going to take it. Of course I would love to have played more but it’s too late to talk about that. I try to do my talking on the pitch and it’s done now, move on. I’m fit.” On Thomas Tuchel “I think that’s just part of the game. When you win there’s going to be noise, when you win there’s going to be noise, it’s how you react to it, how you use it as fuel and today we finished strong and that’s all we could really do and we did that.” Michael Olise *broke* the assists record set by Pele, by the way. He got seven, in more matches, admittedly. Dougal gets in touch: “Not wishing to pour fuel on the fire, but….Bellingham scores and runs straight to the England bench to celebrate with the Jude-whisperer, Jordan Henderson. Cut to Tuchel, sitting on the bench. Not exactly close are they, the star player and his manager? Nice match but defending not brilliant. Cheers for all the great reportage, John.” Here’s the match report on this most ludicrous of matches. Andrew Harper, a voice of reason amid madness: “After the abuse meted out this week it needs to be said for the record - Thomas Tuchel has improved on Southgate and is England’s most successful coach since Alf Ramsey, if you use the not-unreasonable metric of performance at a World Cup. Fair’s fair.” Kari Tulinius gets in touch: “Such an absurd and joyous spectacle! France played in the two best and most entertaining matches at the last two world cups, and it will take some final tomorrow for France not to extend their streak by one year. That said, it was Argentina that was their opponent in the 4-3 in 2018 and the 3-3 in ‘22. What a legacy for Deschamps!” The records that fell: the highest scoring game since 1982, Kylian Mbappe becoming the highest World Cup scorer in history, Bukayo Saka joining the World Cup hat-trick club, Jude Bellingham becoming the first Englishman to score seven World Cup goals, Michael Olise equalling Pele’s assist record for a World Cup. So there’s that. Can a third-place play-off be the best match of a tournament? The answer? Yes. The game that means nothing was one of the highlights of the tournament with records falling over the place. It ends with Thomas Tuchel and Didier Deschamps hugging with huge smiles. That was truly amazing. This is real desperation. England cannot seem to hold on to the ball aside from one man: Jude Bellingham goes on and on, and cuts in and out, beating Lacroix, righting himself and scoring. That’s his seventh goal… Rayan Cherki, canned at half-time, has a haunted expression on the touchline. Jude Bellingham does some useful ball-carrying…but then loses it and Dembele, cutting in from the right, gets to an Upamecano pass and whips the ball home. 90+5 min: Dembele and Olise resume their rhythm and England are sat back deep, with Djed Spence making a clearance before then going down heavily. It looks like he’s pulled something. England down to 10. 90+3 min: The ref does England a solid by getting in the way when the ball is set back for a France shot. Trevoh Chalobah is coming on, the late replacement being a late replacement. Only Kobbie Mainoo with no minutes of the outfield players. 90+1 min: Seven minutes seems low. As Darren Fletcher told us, the ref needed a “rub down” in the second half. 90 min: England remain open to French attacks. There’s plenty of football left to be played. Barcola forces a corner. There will be seven minutes added. Gusto goes off, Kounde on for France. 88 min: Will that decide it? That seems unlikely. How did England get back in a winning position? By attacking. So then, about Atlanta, and those French misses. 87 min: It should be noted that Bellingham passed up the chance to score his seventh goal, the most ever by an Englishman at a World Cup. Saka follows Hurst, Lineker and Kane as an Englishman with a hat-trick. It looks like Bellingham will take it, but then the ball is with Saka, for his hat-trick. The run-up is short but the penalty is true and to the right. 85 min: Spence gallops onwards, and Gusto slides in and catches him. A definite penalty. 84 min: Anderson chases gamely, but France look so much more full of zest. Mbappe skids a shot from distance that Henderso
chineseTitle: 法国 4-6 英格兰：世界杯季军附加赛——事实如此
chineseSummary: 布卡约·萨卡 (Bukayo Saka) 上演帽子戏法，随后裘德·贝林厄姆 (Jude Bellingham) 惊险绝杀，英格兰队获得铜牌
chineseContent: 干得好，英格兰队，再见迪迪埃·德尚，历史上最伟大的国际教练之一。英格兰队球员被授予铜牌。青铜要回家了。司仪乔丹·亨德森 (Jordan Henderson) 呼叫替补门将杰森·斯蒂尔 (Jason Steele)，并向他颁发了一枚奖章。托马斯·图切尔还接受了英国广播公司的采访：“我们上半场表现出色，下半场则动荡不安。你可以看到一天的表现和赛程安排的不同。过去几周我们非常非常疲惫。“这是对我们表现出的心态的高度赞扬和充分尊重。经历过所有的逆境绝对是辉煌的。我担心比赛对身体的要求。我们知道法国的质量和速度，而且他们的日程要少得多。要求较低。 “他们在半决赛之间多了一天的时间来消化，而且他们的旅行距离比我们少得多。我们在炎热的高海拔地区比赛。我很担心身体。你可以在下半场看到它，抽筋和所有的疲劳，但我从不担心心态。我之前说过，这支球队创造了一些非常特别的东西，他们再次展示了这一点。”布卡约·萨卡随后对 BBC 表示：“这是一场非常非常疯狂的比赛。我们都对未能进入决赛感到非常失望，但这是为了强势结束比赛，让我们的国家在世界杯上取得 60 年来最好的成绩，所以我们对最终结果感到满意。 “我认为上半场对我们来说很有利。我们赢了上半场，他们赢了下半场，最后我们进了两个球赢得了比赛，所以我想说比赛就是这样进行的。” “裘德从来没有接受过。他是第一个说去上演帽子戏法的人，所以没有人来分散注意力。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
